1: Therefore, having this ministry by the mercy of God, we do not lose heart (ouk enkakounen) — enkakeō (to grow weary, lose heart, become cowardly) was used in military contexts for desertion under pressure. Paul's apostolic persistence reflects the mercy (eleos) that constitutes the ministry, not personal heroism.
2: But we have renounced disgraceful, underhanded ways — the vocabulary of concealment (kryptō, to hide) and cunning (panourgia, craftiness) that Paul rejects here mirrors the accusations made against him by the super-apostles (2 Corinthians 11:3; 2 Corinthians 12:16). Paul's response: we commend ourselves to everyone's conscience before God.
3: And even if our gospel is veiled, it is veiled to those who are perishing — the veil language from chapter 3 continues; those who cannot see the gospel are not victims of God's arbitrary choice but those who are perishing (apollymenoi) by their own rejection.
4: In their case the god of this age (ho theos tou aiōnos toutou) has blinded the minds of the unbelievers — the title god of this age for Satan reflects Jewish apocalyptic categories (1 Enoch 2 Corinthians 54:5; Testament of Job 23:1-2) where the present evil age is under the influence of hostile spiritual powers. The term ho archōn tou kosmou (ruler of this world) appears in John 12:31; 2 Corinthians 14:30; 2 Corinthians 16:11.
5: For what we proclaim is not ourselves, but Jesus Christ as Lord, with ourselves as your servants (douloi) for Jesus's sake — the contrast between self-proclamation (heautous kēryssomen) and Christ-proclamation defines the apostolic task. The servant/doulos language inverts the Roman imperial patron-client hierarchy.
6: For God, who said let light shine out of darkness, has shone in our hearts — the creation account (Genesis 1:3: let there be light) provides the analogy for the new creation event of conversion. The new creation is as real and as powerful as the original creation; both are acts of divine speech into darkness.
7: But we have this treasure in jars of clay (en ostrakinois skeuesin), to show that the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us — ostrakinos (made of clay/pottery) was used for the cheapest, most common storage vessels. The ostraka (pottery shards) were also used in Athens for voting in the ostracism (exile) procedure, giving us the English word ostracize.
8: We are afflicted in every way, but not crushed; perplexed, but not driven to despair — the four antitheses in 2 Corinthians 4:8-9 follow a regular pattern: thlibomenoi (pressed) but not stenochorumenoi (cramped); aporoumenoi (at a loss) but not exaporoumenoi (utterly at a loss). The second term in each pair intensifies the first: Paul nearly reaches the extreme but does not cross it.
9: Persecuted, but not forsaken; struck down, but not destroyed — the language of violent assault that stops just short of the lethal. The divine protection that prevents the fatal blow is the point: apostolic suffering reaches its limit at the boundary God establishes.
10: Always carrying in the body the dying of Jesus (tēn nekrōsin tou Iesou) — nekrōsis (dying, mortification) is Paul's term for the death-process of crucifixion that continues in apostolic suffering. The phrase in the body emphasizes the physical reality of this participation in Christ's dying.
11: For we who live are always being given over to death for Jesus's sake, so that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our mortal flesh — the double manifestation: death manifested through suffering; life manifested through resurrection power. The mortal flesh (thnētē sarx) is the specific location of divine life's display.
12: So death is at work in us, but life in you — the pastoral economy of apostolic suffering: Paul's death-in-the-body generates life in the congregation. This is not mystical speculation but pastoral observation of what actually happened at Corinth.
13: Since we have the same spirit of faith according to what has been written, I believed, and so I spoke — Paul cites Psalms 116:10 (LXX: I believed, therefore I spoke). Psalm 116 is a thanksgiving psalm for deliverance from death; Paul reads it as the apostle's own experience of believing-through-suffering.
14: Knowing that he who raised the Lord Jesus will raise us also with Jesus and bring us with you into his presence — the resurrection as the practical basis of apostolic endurance. The we/you (Paul and the Corinthians) brought together before God describes the eschatological gathering of the congregation in the presence of Christ.
15: For it is all for your sake, so that as grace extends to more and more people it may increase thanksgiving, to the glory of God — the missionary expansion of grace (charis) generates an expanding multiplication of thanksgiving (eucharistia); Paul frames apostolic mission as the increase of divine worship.
16: So we do not lose heart. Though our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed day by day — the outer/inner distinction (ho exō hēmōn anthrōpos/ho esō hēmōn) reflects the Hellenistic philosophical distinction between the body and the soul/mind. Paul accepts the distinction but transforms it: the inner renewal is through the Spirit, not through philosophical discipline.
17: For this light momentary affliction is preparing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison — the Greek wordplay between the lightness (elaphron) of present affliction and the weight (baros) of glory inverts physical intuition. The eternal weight of glory (aiōnion baros doxēs) is an oxymoron in Greek: heavy eternity vs. light temporality.
18: As we look not to the things that are seen but to the things that are unseen — the visible/invisible distinction maps onto the temporal/eternal distinction. The unseen is the permanent; the seen is the transient. Hebrews 11:1-3 develops the same insight: faith perceives the invisible reality that underlies the visible world.
